content.text: Security IN the Cloud — Customer Responsibilities
AWS secures the infrastructure; you secure everything you deploy on it. "Security IN the cloud" covers six domains:
identity and access management
network configuration
data encryption
operating system and application security
audit and detection
, and
data classification and access control
. Neglecting any one of these domains creates exploitable gaps regardless of how strong the underlying AWS infrastructure security is.

attrs.alt: Customer responsibility areas for security IN the cloud and security maturity spectrum

content.text: Identity and Access Management (IAM)

IAM misconfiguration is the most common root cause of AWS security incidents. The controls are all available — the gap is always in how they are (or are not) configured. Customer responsibility for identity encompasses every decision about who can do what, under what conditions.
Least-privilege policies:

Every IAM principal — user, role, service account — should have exactly the permissions needed to do its job and nothing more. This is achievable in AWS via IAM policy Actions lists, Resource ARN constraints, and Condition keys. In practice, teams often start with broad permissions and never tighten them; the result is an IAM configuration where a compromised Lambda function has full S3 write access to all buckets.
Eliminate long-lived access keys:

IAM user access keys (AKIA... prefix) are long-lived credentials. If they leak via a public GitHub commit, misconfigured server, or compromised developer machine, they remain valid until manually rotated. Replace access keys with IAM Roles wherever possible: EC2 instance profiles, Lambda execution roles, ECS task roles, GitHub Actions OIDC federation to IAM roles.
MFA enforcement:

Require MFA for all human users via IAM policy conditions (aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent). For the root account, enable a hardware MFA device — not a virtual authenticator app. Root account MFA is the single most impactful security configuration a new AWS account can make.
IAM Identity Center for human access:

Instead of creating per-user IAM users, federate human users through IAM Identity Center (formerly SSO). Users authenticate once with your identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace) and receive temporary credentials scoped to specific accounts and permission sets. No long-lived IAM user keys to manage or rotate.
Permission boundaries:

When delegating IAM administration to teams, use permission boundaries to limit the maximum permissions any role created by that team can have. This prevents a team's admin from accidentally granting AdministratorAccess to a service account.
Network Configuration Security

AWS provides the network plumbing, but you configure the access control rules. A default VPC with no customization is a security liability — every EC2 instance launched into it gets a public IP by default. Production environments require deliberate VPC design.
Never use the default VPC in production:

The default VPC has a CIDR of 172.31.0.0/16, a default security group that allows all outbound and all inbound from the same security group, and subnets that assign public IPs to instances by default. Create dedicated VPCs with explicit CIDR blocks, private subnets for backend tiers, and public subnets only for load balancers and NAT gateways.
Security group rule hygiene:

Security groups are stateful firewalls. Common mistakes: opening 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22 (SSH) or 3389 (RDP) to the internet, allowing all traffic between tiers instead of referencing specific security group IDs, or leaving debug rules that opened ports for a test and were never removed. Use AWS Config rule restricted-ssh to alert when port 22 is open to the internet.
Network access control lists (NACLs):

NACLs provide a second, stateless layer of network filtering at the subnet boundary. Unlike security groups, NACLs require explicit rules for both inbound and outbound traffic (since they're stateless). Use NACLs for broad deny rules — blocking known bad CIDR ranges — and security groups for fine-grained service-to-service controls.
VPC Flow Logs:

Enable VPC Flow Logs to S3 for all production VPCs. Flow logs capture connection metadata (not packet contents) and are essential for forensic investigation after a security incident. Without them, you cannot answer 'what IPs did this compromised instance connect to?'
Data Encryption — At Rest and In Transit

AWS provides encryption mechanisms for every storage and transit path, but using them is the customer's responsibility. Encryption at rest is not universally enabled by default across all AWS services.
Default encryption for S3 buckets:

Since January 2023, S3 applies server-side encryption (SSE-S3) by default to all new objects. However, the choice between SSE-S3 (AWS-managed keys), SSE-KMS (your KMS keys with audit trail), and SSE-C (customer-provided keys) is yours. For regulated data, SSE-KMS with a customer-managed key provides the key policy control and CloudTrail audit trail that compliance frameworks require.
EBS volumes:

EBS encryption is not enabled by default. You can enable account-level default encryption via the EC2 console or API, which causes all new EBS volumes and snapshots to be encrypted with the default KMS key. Do this in every account as a baseline control.
RDS encryption:

RDS encryption must be set at database creation time — you cannot encrypt an existing unencrypted instance in place. The migration path (snapshot → copy with encryption → restore) has a maintenance window. Enable encryption by default via an AWS Config rule that alerts on unencrypted RDS instances.
TLS enforcement:

Enforce HTTPS-only access to S3 buckets via bucket policy (aws:SecureTransport condition). Require TLS 1.2+ on ALB listeners. Use ACM to provision and auto-renew certificates — never deploy self-signed certificates in production.
Secrets rotation:

Hard-coded credentials in environment variables, configuration files, or application code are a critical vulnerability. Store all secrets in AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store SecureString. Enable automatic rotation for database passwords — Secrets Manager handles this without downtime for supported databases.
Operating System and Application Security

The guest OS on EC2 instances, the application code you deploy, and the dependencies your applications use are entirely your responsibility. AWS provides no guest OS patching, application scanning, or runtime protection — those tools exist, but you must deploy and configure them.
EC2 patching with SSM Patch Manager:

AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager automates OS patching across a fleet of EC2 instances without requiring SSH access or a bastion host. Define a patch baseline (which patches to apply), a maintenance window (when), and a patch group (which instances). SSM Agent (pre-installed on AWS-provided AMIs) applies patches and reports compliance status.
Container image scanning:

ECR provides both basic scanning (free, uses Clair) and enhanced scanning (Inspector-based, continuous CVE monitoring). Enable enhanced scanning and set a policy to block deployment of images with HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs from reaching production. This is a pipeline gate, not a manual process.
Dependency security:

Third-party libraries introduce vulnerabilities. Integrate tools like Snyk, Dependabot, or OWASP Dependency-Check into your CI/CD pipeline to detect known CVEs in npm, pip, Maven, and Go module dependencies before they reach production.
Principle of minimal containers:

Use distroless or Alpine-based images rather than Ubuntu or Amazon Linux full images for containerized workloads. Fewer installed packages means fewer CVEs. The Dockerfile should copy only the application artifact, not a full development environment.
AWS Inspector for continuous scanning:

Inspector v2 continuously monitors EC2 instances and ECR images for new CVEs as they are published — not just at the time of initial deployment. An image that was clean at push can be flagged two months later when a new CVE is published against one of its components.

# SSM Patch Manager: apply patches immediately to a patch group
aws ssm send-command \
  --document-name "AWS-RunPatchBaseline" \
  --targets "Key=tag:PatchGroup,Values=production" \
  --parameters "Operation=Install,RebootOption=RebootIfNeeded" \
  --comment "Emergency patch: CVE-2024-XXXXX"

# Check patch compliance across the fleet
aws ssm describe-instance-patch-states-for-patch-group \
  --patch-group "production" \
  --query "InstancePatchStates[?MissingCount>`0`].{ID:InstanceId,Missing:MissingCount}"
Audit, Detection, and Incident Response

Security controls you cannot observe are security controls you cannot trust. Audit and detection capabilities are customer-configured — AWS does not alert you when your S3 bucket goes public or when unusual API calls originate from your account unless you configure the detection services.
CloudTrail: the minimum requirement:

Every production AWS account must have a multi-region CloudTrail trail delivering logs to an S3 bucket in a separate, locked-down logging account. Without CloudTrail, you cannot investigate a security incident — you have no record of who called which API. Enable CloudTrail log file validation to detect tampering.
GuardDuty: turn it on everywhere:

GuardDuty requires zero configuration beyond enabling it. It analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs automatically. The cost is typically a few dollars per account per month — far less than the cost of any breach it might detect. Designate a GuardDuty administrator account through Organizations and enable it for all member accounts with a few API calls.
Security Hub as the single pane of glass:

Enable Security Hub with the AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard enabled. It aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and IAM Access Analyzer into a single view with a security score. Route high-severity findings to PagerDuty or Slack via EventBridge.
Incident response playbooks:

Detection without response is useless. Document runbooks for the most likely incidents: compromised IAM credentials (revoke, investigate CloudTrail, check for persistence), exposed S3 bucket (enable Block Public Access, review access logs, check for data exfiltration), unauthorized EC2 instance (quarantine, take memory snapshot, analyze with Amazon Detective).
Data Classification and Access Control
What data you store, how sensitive it is, and who can access it are entirely customer decisions. AWS provides the tools; the policies are yours to define and enforce.
S3 Block Public Access:

Enable Block Public Access at the account level — this single setting prevents any S3 bucket or object from being made publicly accessible, regardless of individual bucket ACLs or policies. This should be the default for every new AWS account. A public S3 bucket containing customer data is entirely a customer misconfiguration, not an AWS failure.
Data classification policy:

Define data classification tiers for your organization (e.g., Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted) and map them to AWS controls. Restricted data requires customer-managed KMS keys, CloudTrail data event logging, VPC endpoints so data never traverses the internet, and Macie monitoring.
Resource-based policies:

S3 bucket policies, KMS key policies, SQS queue policies, and Lambda resource policies grant access to specific principals. Audit these periodically with IAM Access Analyzer, which identifies any resource reachable from outside your AWS account or organization.
S3 Object Lock for compliance:

For data that must be retained immutably (financial records, audit logs, healthcare data under HIPAA), enable S3 Object Lock in Compliance mode. Objects cannot be deleted or overwritten for the specified retention period — not by any IAM user, not even by root.
Common Customer Security Failures and Their Causes
Failure
Root Cause
Prevention
Public S3 bucket with PII
Block Public Access not enabled
Enable account-level BPA on day one
Compromised EC2 via exposed SSH
Port 22 open to 0.0.0.0/0 in security group
Use SSM Session Manager; no SSH
Leaked IAM access key
Key committed to GitHub repo
No long-lived keys; use OIDC roles
Lateral movement after breach
Overly permissive IAM roles
Least privilege; IAM Access Analyzer
Ransomware on unversioned S3
No versioning, no Object Lock
Enable versioning + lifecycle + Object Lock
Credential stuffing on Cognito
No rate limiting or CAPTCHA
WAF rate-based rule on auth endpoints
Undetected breach (months)
GuardDuty/CloudTrail not enabled
Enable detective controls day one
Building a Security Baseline: Minimum Controls for Any AWS Account
For any new AWS account, these controls should be enabled before deploying any workloads. They represent the absolute minimum security posture — not a comprehensive security program.
Enable MFA on the root account:
Use a hardware MFA device. Do not use root for day-to-day operations after this setup.
Delete root account access keys:
Run:
aws iam delete-access-key --access-key-id <ROOT_KEY_ID>
. Root access keys should never exist.
Enable CloudTrail in all regions:
Create a multi-region trail delivering to an S3 bucket with versioning and MFA delete enabled.
Enable GuardDuty:
Takes 30 seconds. Costs a few dollars per month. Provides immediate threat detection across the account.
Enable Security Hub with FSBP standard:
Gives you a security score and actionable findings immediately.
Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level:
Prevents accidental public bucket exposure.
Enable EBS default encryption:
All new volumes will be encrypted using the default KMS key.
Set a budget alert:
Not security, but an unexpected bill is often the first sign of a compromised account spinning up mining instances.

#!/bin/bash
# Minimum security baseline for a new AWS account
REGION="us-east-1"

# Enable GuardDuty
aws guardduty create-detector --enable --finding-publishing-frequency FIFTEEN_MINUTES

# Enable Security Hub
aws securityhub enable-security-hub --enable-default-standards

# Block S3 public access account-wide
aws s3control put-public-access-block \
  --account-id $(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Account --output text) \
  --public-access-block-configuration \
  "BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true"

# Enable EBS default encryption
aws ec2 enable-ebs-encryption-by-default --region $REGION
Multi-Account Strategy as a Security Control

Account-level isolation is one of the most effective and underused security controls in AWS. A security boundary between AWS accounts is stronger than any IAM policy — an IAM policy grants access within an account, but a cross-account access attempt requires explicit trust in both the calling and receiving accounts. Separating workloads across accounts reduces blast radius when a credential is compromised.
Account per environment:

Keep development, staging, and production in separate AWS accounts. A developer with broad permissions in dev cannot accidentally modify production resources — there is no IAM path across the account boundary without explicit cross-account role trust.
Security tooling account:

Run GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail log aggregation, and Config aggregator in a dedicated security account. This account has read access to other accounts but no write access. Compromising a workload account does not give an attacker access to security monitoring data.
Log archive account:

Centralize CloudTrail, Config, and VPC Flow Log delivery to an S3 bucket in a dedicated log archive account. The bucket policy prevents deletion. Even with Administrator access in a workload account, an attacker cannot delete the audit trail because they cannot access the log archive account.
AWS Control Tower:

AWS Control Tower automates multi-account setup with pre-configured security guardrails (preventive SCPs + detective Config rules), a landing zone structure (Management, Log Archive, Audit accounts), and enrollment for new accounts. It is the recommended starting point for organizations managing more than 5 accounts.
Infrastructure as Code Security (Shift Left)

Security configurations that live in manually applied settings are fragile — they get overridden by hand, inconsistently applied across regions, or forgotten during incident response. Expressing security controls as code (Terraform, CDK, CloudFormation) makes them reproducible, reviewable, and auditable.
IaC scanning in CI/CD:

Run static analysis tools (Checkov, tfsec, cfn-nag) on Terraform or CloudFormation before applying changes. These tools catch common security misconfigurations — public S3 buckets, security groups open to 0.0.0.0/0, unencrypted EBS volumes — before they ever reach AWS.
Immutable infrastructure:

Never SSH into a running EC2 instance to make configuration changes. Instead, bake changes into a new AMI (with Packer), deploy it alongside the existing fleet, and terminate old instances. This eliminates configuration drift and ensures that every instance in production matches the audited IaC definition.
OPA and Sentinel for policy as code:

Open Policy Agent (OPA) and HashiCorp Sentinel enforce organizational policies at the Terraform plan stage — before any resource is created. For example: 'all S3 buckets must have versioning enabled' or 'KMS customer-managed keys required for any database'.
Drift detection:

AWS Config continuously detects when a resource's actual configuration drifts from its CloudFormation stack definition. CloudFormation Drift Detection identifies resources that were changed outside of IaC. Alert on drift and treat it as a security finding — manual changes bypass your review and approval process.

# checkov scan: catch misconfigurations before apply
checkov -d ./terraform/ --framework terraform \
  --check CKV_AWS_21,CKV_AWS_18,CKV_AWS_19  # S3: versioning, logging, encryption

# tfsec: another popular IaC scanner
tfsec ./terraform/ --exclude-checks aws-iam-no-policy-wildcards

# In GitHub Actions CI pipeline:
# - name: Security scan
#   uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@master
#   with:
#     directory: terraform/
#     soft_fail: false  # block PR merge on HIGH severity
Security in Shared Services and Third-Party Integrations

Modern applications use dozens of third-party services — payment processors, analytics platforms, CRM systems, SaaS tools. Each integration is a potential attack vector if credentials or data are handled carelessly.
Limit third-party AWS access:

When a vendor needs access to your AWS account (a monitoring tool, a billing optimizer, a security scanner), create a cross-account IAM role with the minimum permissions required and a strict External ID condition. Review and audit vendor IAM roles quarterly — vendors you no longer use often retain access.
Webhook security:

Inbound webhooks from third-party services (Stripe payment events, GitHub CI notifications, Salesforce change events) should be validated with HMAC signatures before processing. Never trust the payload without verifying it came from the expected source.
VPC PrivateLink for SaaS:

Some SaaS providers (Snowflake, Datadog, various AWS Marketplace vendors) support VPC PrivateLink, allowing your VPC to reach their service over a private ENI without exposing the connection to the internet. Use PrivateLink for services that process sensitive data.
API key management:

API keys for third-party services (Stripe, Twilio, SendGrid) should be stored in Secrets Manager, never in environment variables in Lambda function configurations or ECS task definitions where they appear in plaintext in the AWS Console.
Key Takeaways
The responsibility boundary is clear — apply it consistently:

Every resource visible in the AWS Console is customer-managed. Every resource below the hypervisor is AWS-managed. When configuring a resource, ask: 'If I misconfigure this, who is responsible for the breach?' The answer is always you.
IAM is the highest-leverage control:

The majority of significant AWS breaches trace to IAM misconfiguration — leaked access keys, overly permissive roles, or no MFA on privileged accounts. Getting IAM right (roles over users, least privilege, MFA everywhere, no long-lived keys) prevents an entire category of attacks.
Enable detective controls before you need them:

CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and VPC Flow Logs cannot be retroactively applied to an incident that occurred before they were enabled. Turn them on in every account on day one — they are cheap relative to the cost of flying blind during an incident.
Encryption is opt-in for most services:
Enable account-level S3 encryption, EBS default encryption, and use KMS CMKs for regulated data. Audit with AWS Config rules that flag unencrypted resources before a breach, not after.
Automate security hygiene:

Manual audits are periodic; threats are continuous. AWS Config rules, Security Hub standards, and automated remediation via Systems Manager turn security checks into continuous controls rather than quarterly checkbox exercises.
